calculatorHTML:
Quilt Backing Calculator

Backing Yardage
—
Seams Needed
—

Backing Layout

howItWorks.intro: Backing has to finish larger than the quilt top on every side so the longarm frame has fabric to grip and so nothing runs short if the layers shift during quilting. Once the target backing exceeds the usable width of your fabric — about 42–44 inches for standard quilting cotton — the panel has to be pieced from two or three widths seamed together.
rules: Add 4 inches of overhang on each side, so the backing measures 8 inches wider and 8 inches longer than the top
If the target backing width is more than the usable fabric width, you seam two or three widths together to reach it
On 42–44" cotton, any quilt wider than roughly 36" needs at least one seam; a 108" wide back covers most beds seamless
howItWorks.outro: The calculator compares your target backing size against the fabric width, decides how many widths it takes, runs the seam either vertically or horizontally to use less fabric, and totals the yardage for you.

faq.question: How much bigger than the quilt top should the backing be?
faq.answer: Aim for 4 inches of extra backing on each side, which makes the backing 8 inches wider and 8 inches longer than the top. So a 60×80" top wants backing around 68×88". Many longarm quilters ask for a full 4–6 inches per side because their clamps and leaders eat into the edges — always confirm the number your quilter wants before you cut.

faq.question: When do I have to piece the backing instead of using one width?
faq.answer: You piece as soon as your target backing dimension is wider than the usable fabric. Standard quilting cotton gives about 42–44" once you trim the selvages, so a backing that needs to be 68" wide can't come from a single width. Two widths seamed together give you roughly 84–88" of usable width, which covers throws and twins; three widths handle queen and king backs. A 108" wide backing avoids seams on almost everything.

faq.question: Should the seam run horizontally or vertically?
faq.answer: Run the seam whichever direction uses less fabric. For a 68×88" backing from 44" cotton, two vertical widths of 88" each is about 176" (≈5 yards), while two horizontal widths of 68" each is about 136" (≈3.8 yards) — the horizontal layout wins here. The calculator tries both orientations and reports the cheaper one. Press the seam open and keep it off-center so it doesn't land under a heavily quilted block.

faq.question: What is wide backing fabric and when is it worth it?
faq.answer: Wide backing is quilting cotton woven at 108" (3 yards) wide specifically so you can back most quilts with a single seamless panel. A queen backing of 94×104" needs just about 3 yards of 108" fabric flat, versus three seamed widths of 44" cotton totaling roughly 8.7 yards. It costs more per yard but you skip the seaming, and there's no seam ridge for the longarm to bump over.

faq.question: Do I calculate batting the same way as backing?
faq.answer: Yes — batting matches the backing, not the top. Cut or buy batting at least 4 inches bigger per side, so a 60×80" top takes batting of at least 68×88". Batting comes in standard package sizes (crib 45×60", twin 72×90", full 81×96", queen 90×108", king 120×120"), so round up to the next size that covers your number rather than buying exactly to fit.

faq.question: How much backing does a king quilt take?
faq.answer: A king top runs about 104×96". With 4" overhang per side that's 112×104" of backing. From 44" cotton you need three widths of 112" each — roughly 9.3 yards — sewn with two seams. From 108" wide backing you need about 104" of length turned sideways, close to 3 yards with no seams at all. The wide backing is usually the better buy at king size.

faq.question: Should I pre-wash backing fabric?
faq.answer: If you'll wash the finished quilt and want it to stay flat, pre-wash the backing so it shrinks before it's quilted in — most quilting cotton shrinks 3–5%, and unshrunk backing against a washed top can pucker. If you're after the crinkled, antique look, skip pre-washing and let everything shrink together in the first wash.

chart.title: Standard Quilt Sizes & Backing Yardage
chart.subtitle: Yardage includes 4-inch overhang per side. Based on 44-inch fabric width.

howWeCalculate.intro: The math follows what a longarm quilter actually checks in when you drop off a top: add the overhang, compare against the fabric width, and count widths.
steps: Add twice your per-side overhang to both the width and the length of the quilt top — 4" per side adds 8" each way
Compare that target width to the usable fabric width (about 42–44" for cotton, or 108" for wide backing)
Divide the target width by the usable width and round up to get the number of fabric widths, then multiply by the target length for total inches
Convert inches to yards (÷36) for each seam orientation and report the layout that uses the least fabric
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: Yardage is a starting estimate. Buy an extra quarter-yard for squaring up and shrinkage, and confirm the overhang your longarm quilter requires before cutting.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this when you've finished a quilt top and need to know how much backing and batting to buy before the fabric store or before a longarm drop-off.
scenarios: Working out how many 44" widths to seam together for a backing wider than the bolt
Buying backing yardage at the shop and needing the number before you get to the cutting counter
Prepping a top for a longarm quilter who asked for a specific overhang per side
Deciding whether 108" wide backing is cheaper than piecing several widths of regular cotton
Sizing batting to match the backing so both extend past the top on every side

sections.title: What This Calculator Figures Out
sections.body: Enter your quilt top width and length, pick the fabric width you're buying, and set the overhang, and this tool returns the finished backing size, how many fabric widths to seam, the batting size, and the total yardage. The backing has to end up larger than the top on every side because the quilt sandwich shifts as it's quilted and the longarm frame needs fabric to clamp. The usable width of your fabric decides whether one width does the job or whether you seam two or three together.

sections.title: A Worked Example
sections.body: Take a common 60×80" throw or twin top with 4" of overhang on each side. The backing target becomes 68×88". Since 68" is well past the 44" usable width of quilting cotton, one width won't reach — you seam two. Two widths of 88" run about 176" total, close to 5 yards; flipping the seam horizontal to two 68" widths drops it to about 136", roughly 3.8 yards. A single 108" wide backing would cover the 68" width flat and need only the 88" length, about 2.5 yards with no seam.

sections.title: Why the Fabric Width Drives Everything
sections.body: Standard quilting cotton is sold at 42–44" but you lose the selvages, so plan on 42" usable. That's why any backing wider than roughly 36" already forces a seam once you add overhang. Piecing isn't a flaw — a well-placed horizontal seam often uses far less fabric than stacking vertical widths, and pressing it open keeps the back flat for quilting. When the seaming math gets expensive, that's the signal to price out 108" wide backing instead.

howToSteps.title: Measure the Quilt Top
howToSteps.description: Enter the finished width and length of your quilt top in inches. Measure the actual top, not the bed size, since tops often differ from mattress dimensions.

howToSteps.title: Choose the Backing Fabric Width
howToSteps.description: Pick 42–44" for standard quilting cotton or 108" if you're buying wide backing. This is what decides whether the backing needs seams.

howToSteps.title: Set the Overhang per Side
howToSteps.description: Use 4" per side for most projects, or the number your longarm quilter asked for — some want 4–6" to clamp onto.

howToSteps.title: Read the Layout and Yardage
howToSteps.description: The results show the finished backing size, how many widths to seam and in which direction, the batting size, and total yards to buy.

tips: Measure the actual finished top rather than the bed size — pieced tops shrink a bit at every seam and often come out smaller than planned.
When two widths give you a lot of leftover, try flipping the seam direction; a horizontal seam frequently uses a yard less than stacking vertical panels.
Trim the selvages off before seaming — leaving them in creates a tight, wavy seam that fights the longarm.
Buy an extra quarter-yard beyond the calculated number for squaring up the backing and absorbing shrinkage.
Place the backing seam off-center so it doesn't line up under a densely quilted block, where the extra bulk shows most.
